analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 4 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: jtag_unlock acts as a global lock reset, bypassing lock protection for all peripherals.
findings.vulnerability_category: Assertion of jtag_unlock signal (or debug_mode_i) that clears or bypasses peripheral protections / reglk_ctrl lock registers (via reglk_wrapper reset), and potentially AES internal protections via debug_mode_i / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Reset condition if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)) resets all reglk_mem bits to 0.
dm_master_gnt = '0; debug AXI master tied off.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Debug/JTAG interface (dmi_jtag, dm_top) and its jtag_unlock output Operation: Assertion of jtag_unlock signal (or debug_mode_i) that clears or bypasses peripheral protections Object: reglk_ctrl lock registers (via reglk_wrapper reset), and potentially AES internal protections via debug_mode_i Expected guard: Debug should not be able to clear lock bits or bypass access control without explicit authenticated unlock sequence Observed behavior: jtag_unlock directly participates in reglk_wrapper reset condition: ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9) resets ALL lock bits to 0, clearing all peripheral locks. The direct debug-to-peripheral AXI path (dm_master) is tied off, but the jtag_unlock sideband signal bypasses the lock mechanism. Additionally, debug_mode_i is connected to AES wrappers; internal bypass may exist there. Path: dmi_jtag → jtag_unlock → reglk_wrapper reset condition → reglk_mem[j] ≤ 0 Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: All peripheral lock bits cleared; all peripherals become writable/readable regardless of prior lock state.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full bypass effect inside AES wrappers from debug_mode_i unknown; AES wrapper RTL not inspected.

findings.summary: Peripheral write paths lack lock gating; hardware lock mechanism not enforced for data registers.
findings.vulnerability_category: Write to start_reg, length_reg, source/dest address registers after lock has been set / DMA configuration registers (start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb/msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb/msb_reg, end_reg) and PKT fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o registers / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Write case: else if(en && we) ... case(address) ... no core_lock_reg check for start_reg, length_reg, etc.
core_lock_reg write at address 7 does not gate other writes.
Write case: else if(en && we) ... no lock check for fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o.
reglk_ctrl_i input declared but unused in write always block.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any AXI initiator writing to DMA configuration registers or PKT control registers Operation: Write to start_reg, length_reg, source/dest address registers after lock has been set Object: DMA configuration registers (start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb/msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb/msb_reg, end_reg) and PKT fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o registers Expected guard: we_final = we && !core_lock_reg && !reglk_ctrl_dma_bit for DMA; similar lock gating for PKT accesses Observed behavior: In dma_wrapper, the write enable logic (en && we) does not include any check of core_lock_reg or reglk_ctrl_i. core_lock_reg is a write-once register but its value is never used as a write gate. In pkt_wrapper, writes to fuse_req/fuse_addr also lack any lock check. reglk_ctrl_i is an input but is not referenced in the write always block. Path: ariane_axi → axi_lite_interface → en && we → case(address) direct register write without lock condition Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: DMA can be reprogrammed (new source/dest/length) even after locking; allows memory protection bypass via rogue DMA transfers. PKT fuse controls can be modified.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether reglk_ctrl_i is intended to be checked in wrapper but omitted.

findings.summary: DMA core_lock_reg does not enforce lock on configuration registers; lock is ineffective.
findings.vulnerability_category: Write to DMA control registers (start_reg, length_reg, source/dest addr) after core_lock_reg is set / DMA configuration registers: start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg, end_reg / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Write case: else if(en && we) ... case(address) ... 0: start_reg <= wdata; 1: length_reg <= wdata; ... no lock check.
Write to core_lock_reg at address 7: core_lock_reg <= (wdata==0) ? 0 : ((core_lock_reg==0) ? wdata : 0); core_lock_reg is not used as gate.
reglk_ctrl_i input not used in write path.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software/debug writes to DMA configuration registers Operation: Write to DMA control registers (start_reg, length_reg, source/dest addr) after core_lock_reg is set Object: DMA configuration registers: start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg, end_reg Expected guard: When core_lock_reg is non-zero, writes to those registers must be blocked. The write enable should include !core_lock_reg. Observed behavior: The write logic in dma_wrapper uses only en && we; no core_lock_reg gate exists. The core_lock_reg is reachable via address 7 and is write-once, but its value is never used to condition writes to other registers. reglk_ctrl_i is an input but is not used in the write always block. Path: ariane_axi → axi_lite_interface → en && we → case(address) direct register updates without lock check Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: DMA configuration can be altered after locking, enabling unauthorized DMA transfers to/from protected memory regions.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No evidence of any other lock mechanisms in the full DMA engine; sideband debug/test path not visible.

findings.summary: Lock and access-control registers reset to permissive state, violating safe-default requirement.
findings.vulnerability_category: Reset assertion, setting initial values for lock and access-control registers / reglk_ctrl lock bits (via reglk_mem) and acc_ctrl access-control matrix (via acct_mem) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Reset inputs (rst_ni, rst_6, rst_9, etc.) Operation: Reset assertion, setting initial values for lock and access-control registers Object: reglk_ctrl lock bits (via reglk_mem) and acc_ctrl access-control matrix (via acct_mem) Expected guard: Reset must set reglk_ctrl to all-ones (locked) and acc_ctrl to all-zeros (deny all). Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper resets reglk_mem to 0 when reset condition is true (rst_ni=0 or rst_9=1), meaning all lock bits are cleared (unlocked). acct_wrapper resets acct_mem to 32'hffffffff (all access allowed). Both reset to permissive states. Path: rst_ni or rst_9 assertion → reglk_mem <= 0; rst_ni or rst_6 assertion → acct_mem <= 32'hffffffff Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: System boots with all peripherals unlocked and accessible; secure boot must reconfigure locks before any untrusted code runs, creating a window of vulnerability.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether production reset sequence holds rst_6/rst_9 high after rst_ni deassertion is not fully determined, but the reset condition clears locks during reset.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
